---
# Source Card: Hematuria and Proteinuria in Children

## Summary
This peer-reviewed AAP review (Pediatrics in Review, 2018) establishes operational, age-banded definitions for pediatric hematuria and proteinuria used in primary-care screening and referral. It supplies numeric cutoffs the pediatric CDS module can carry as source-supported facts: microscopic/persistent hematuria RBC counts, hypercalciuria urine calcium/creatinine ratios (with distinct infant bands), the proteinuria and nephrotic-range proteinuria definitions (mg/m2/day, mg/kg/day, and U p/c), and the orthostatic-proteinuria diagnostic pattern. Thresholds are the authors' practice definitions synthesizing prior school-age screening studies, not a single primary guideline.

## Key evidence (with exact-passage locators)
- (ev_001) Microscopic hematuria = >5 RBC/HPF centrifuged (or >5 RBC/mm3 uncentrifuged) on 2-3 occasions; literature ranges 1-10 RBC/HPF — locator: Definitions and Measurement Methods — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:2383865954c7b88d84c80f827f6ac7cebd536b8ac8e16594d962621845d6c35b]"
- (ev_002) Persistent hematuria = >4-6 weeks of >5 RBC/HPF absent exercise/menses/trauma — locator: Classification — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:fff684a625920f95194c2d5d3bed019ffde7cba6818474f3f12f0ed33075b4cb]"
- (ev_003) Hypercalciuria (>2 yr) = U ca/cr >0.2 mg/mg; infants <6 mo acceptable <0.8, 6-12 mo <0.6 — locator: Nonglomerular Causes of Hematuria — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:4f7500633075d472726a4dc4d8bde62ea1c95d6d6c8050823a0b7f7d2409cb14]"
- (ev_004) Proteinuria = >100 mg/m2/day or U p/c >0.2 (>2 yr); infants 6-24 mo normal U p/c <0.5; neonates/infants up to 300 mg/m2 allowed — locator: Definition / Measurement Methods — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:4190640315011cf285c87ee1875f978f678db6104fe8693c7ca2e492642f6c27]"
- (ev_005) Nephrotic-range proteinuria = >1,000 mg/m2/day, or >50 mg/kg/day, or U p/c >2 — locator: Definition — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:1afa6d9c8397c650b6efe8a1762955b3c4b64678a2fd243e888a3fe02378c907]"
- (ev_006) Orthostatic proteinuria diagnosed when first-morning U p/c <=0.2 with random U p/c >0.2; common and benign in adolescents — locator: Classification — quote: "[redacted — content-rights: restricted (usage.allowed_for_public_output=false); sha256:b3839fb7ecc7bae3c0096642c8be2b6698276671d90e24b668f1bf8566e4b4d6]"

## Population / assay / lifecycle notes
- Age-banding is central: several cutoffs differ by band. Hypercalciuria and proteinuria U p/c normals are explicitly higher in infants than in children >2 years (infant physiology: higher calcium excretion, lower urine creatinine). Neonates/infants tolerate proteinuria up to 300 mg/m2/day.
- Assay/method dependence: microscopy counts differ between centrifuged (per HPF) and uncentrifuged (per mm3) urine; UA-with-microscopy is stated to be laboratory-dependent. Dipstick protein detects albumin as a surrogate and misses LMW proteins. Spot U p/c is a surrogate for 24-hr collection, most reliable on first-morning void; falsely elevated at low creatinine excretion, underestimated in concentrated samples.
- Lifecycle: single effective version — Pediatr Rev 2018;39(12):573-587. No retirement or superseding guideline recorded in this source.

## Scope-exit / referral relevance (if any)
- Yes. The source defines referral/escalation boundaries: persistent proteinuria (first-morning U p/c >0.2) warrants renal ultrasonography, serum creatinine/albumin/cholesterol/electrolytes, C3/C4, ANA, and possible pediatric-nephrology referral; a first-morning U p/c <=0.2 needs no further evaluation (orthostatic). Nephrotic-range proteinuria and persistent hematuria with macroscopic hematuria/proteinuria are flagged as higher-risk. These are referral triggers for a CDS scope boundary, NOT treatment directives.

## Limitations & conflicts
- The published print uses "mg/m2" (superscript 2); the machine text extraction dropped the superscript ("mg/m"). Quotes here reconstruct the superscript to match the source of record — a CDS converter must treat body-surface-area protein thresholds as mg/m2/day, never mg/m/day.
- The 1-10 RBC/HPF literature range is preserved as a stated heterogeneity, not collapsed: the authors adopt >5 RBC/HPF but explicitly acknowledge other definitions exist. A downstream conflict with any source using a different RBC/HPF cutoff must be kept as a conflict, not averaged.
- Thresholds are review-level author practice definitions (secondary source), not a primary society clinical practice guideline; if a primary KDIGO/AAP guideline supplies a differing cutoff it should be marked conflicts_with rather than merged.
- No sex-specific numeric thresholds are given; the 24-hr creatinine adequacy figures (15-20 mg/kg females, 20-25 mg/kg males) are the only sex-differentiated numbers and were not extracted as a CDS threshold point here.
